esidue Theory

$\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x$ exists as a finite number. Similarly, $\int_{-\infty}^{b} f(x) d x$ is $\mathbf{c}$ vergent if $\lim _{a \rightarrow-\infty} \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x$ exists as a finite number.

Now let $f(x)$ be continuous on the real line. The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x$ is also improper since the interval of integration is infinite, but here infinite in both the positive and negative direction. Such an integral is to be convergent if both $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{0} f(x) d x$ are convergent. this case, we set (Figure 1)
ng an imor the line.
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x=\lim _{a \rightarrow-\infty} \int_{a}^{0} f(x) d x+\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{0}^{b} f(x) d x
$$

This expression should be contrasted with the following, which integr the function along expanding symmetric intervals. We define the Cau principal value of the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ to be (Figure 2)
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x=\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x \text {, if the limit exists. }
$$

The Cauchy principal value of an integral may exist even though the int itself is not convergent. For example, $\int_{-a}^{a} x d x=0$ for all $a$, which im that P.V. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x d x=0$, but the integral itself is clearly not convergent $\int_{0}^{\infty} x d x=\infty$.

However, whenever $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ is convergent, then P.V. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d$ ists, and the two integrals will be the same. This is because $\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{-a}^{0}$ and $\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x$ both exist, and so
Cauchy princiintegral.
$$
\text { P.V. } \begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x & =\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x \\
& =\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty}\left(\int_{-a}^{0} f(x) d x+\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x\right) \\
& =\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{-a}^{0} f(x) d x+\lim _{a \rightarrow \infty} \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x \\
& =\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) d x
\end{aligned}
$$
